danger_signals.visual_form: animated dinosaur sprites with distinct creature designs (e.g., pterodactylus)
danger_signals.meaning: immediate threat; unarmed humans will be eaten if within walking range

danger_signals.visual_form: humanoid sprites visually distinct from player tribe, armed with spears
danger_signals.meaning: hostile entities; rival tribal faction that poses combat threat

danger_signals.visual_form: visual depth/height differences in level geometry, impassable spaces
danger_signals.meaning: structural hazard; humans cannot cross without tools or cooperation

danger_signals.visual_form: time remaining displayed in UI at bottom of screen
danger_signals.meaning: resource scarcity state; level must be completed before time expires

safety_signals.visual_form: conspicuous colored tile marking end point location
safety_signals.meaning: safe completion zone; reaching this with at least one human completes level

safety_signals.visual_form: spears, torches, wheels, ropes, witch doctor availability shown in UI action menu
safety_signals.meaning: capacity restored; tribe now has capability to overcome specific obstacle types

safety_signals.visual_form: human sprites visible on screen; tribe count displayed in bottom UI
safety_signals.meaning: resource available; more humans = more tool-stacking and parallel problem-solving options

safety_signals.visual_form: pterodactylus or similar NPC creatures present in level terrain
safety_signals.meaning: safe passage route; can be used to reach otherwise unreachable platforms without player control required

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: humanoid sprite design distinct from enemy tribe members via clothing/appearance variation
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player humans stack together, use tools cooperatively; visual grouping differentiates from isolated enemy tribe members

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: spear-wielding humanoid sprites with distinct visual differentiation from player tribe (likely different coloring or apparel in pixel art)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: positioned as isolated hostile units; branded as adversarial through combat behavior and tool possession

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: dinosaur species vary by level (pterodactylus, generic dinosaurs); size and animation convey threat level
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: positioned in terrain as environmental hazards; behavior-driven identification (patrolling, attacking unarmed humans)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: human sprites appear small, exposed in open spaces; low tribe count displayed in UI
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: humans move cautiously or cluster; dinosaurs more aggressive; enemy tribe members roam uncontested

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: tool icons populate action menu at bottom UI; humans carry visible weapons/items (spears, torches)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: humans can kill dinosaurs, fight enemy tribe members, construct ladders via stacking

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: time remaining counter at bottom UI decreases visibly; level becomes more cluttered/complex as time advances
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player must prioritize goal path over exploration; waste of time results in failure state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: levels themed around prehistoric human evolution; increasingly complex tool sets introduced (spears → torches → wheels → ropes → witch doctor)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: capabilities expand per level; new abilities unlock, reflecting evolutionary advancement of tribe

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: rescue opportunities appear in levels; NPCs or trapped humans visible as interactive elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: tribe count increases when humans rescued; access to larger labor pool for subsequent level attempts

environmental_storytelling.example: prehistoric environment progression communicates evolution theme; early levels feature basic obstacles (gaps, single predators), later levels introduce complex tool requirements and multiple enemy types, reflecting human advancement
spear introduced in first level as foundational tool; subsequent levels layer new tools (torches, wheels, ropes) to expand player capability and suggest technological progress

dinosaur types and enemy tribe presence vary by level, suggesting different environmental/political conditions; presence of both fauna and rival humans creates tension between natural and social threats
colored tile marking end point is visually conspicuous and placed in complex terrain, communicating both objective clarity and challenge magnitude
bottom UI panel shows tribe count, time remaining, and available actions—constant visual reminder of resource scarcity and constraints driving player decision-making

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: dinosaur actively hunts and eats unarmed humans within walking range; indicates predator-prey dynamic tied to tool possession
dinosaur can be killed or temporarily held off by spear-wielding human; indicates power inversion when tribe gains tool advantage
pterodactylus cannot be controlled but can be used to reach platforms; indicates cooperative environmental use without direct command
actively opposes player tribe; patrols territory and attacks; indicates rival faction with equal tool sophistication
multiple humans can stack vertically to reach high ledges; indicates tribal cooperation mechanic tied to visual geometry
spear can be thrown across gaps, used for jumping, or held for combat; tool possession expands available actions and communicates capability expansion

notes: The Humans communicates world-state primarily through level design complexity, tool availability, and enemy variety. Visual communication relies on pixel art sprite differentiation (player tribe vs. enemy tribe vs. fauna), UI bottom-panel resource tracking, and conspicuous goal marker placement. The game's progression from basic obstacles to complex multi-tool scenarios mirrors human evolution thematically. Danger is signaled through dinosaur presence and enemy tribe member activity; safety through goal tiles, tool acquisition, and tribe size. No explicit faction symbols or color-coded allegiances are documented, but visual sprite differentiation and behavior patterns establish clear tribal and predator hierarchies. The sim-strategy paradigm manifests through resource management (tribe count, time, tools) displayed in the UI and expressed through level-by-level puzzle design.
